title: NOC 94130 – Textile fibre and yarn, hide and pelt processing machine operators and workers | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s textile and leather processing industries are undergoing a massive transformation, moving toward high-performance technical fabrics and sustainable, "traceable" leather production. Textile Fibre, Yarn, and Hide Processing Operators (NOC 94130) are the skilled workers who manage the foundational stages of these supply chains. Whether it is preparing carbon-fibre yarns for the aerospace sector or processing high-quality hides for Canada’s luxury automotive interiors, these operators are in steady demand as manufacturers "re-shore" production to North America to ensure quality and sustainability.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group941 – Processing and manufacturing machine operators

duties: Machine Operation: Operating and monitoring machines that prepare textile fibres, spin yarn, or process hides and pelts.
Chemical Treatment: Managing the application of dyes, bleaches, and tanning chemicals to ensure consistent color and durability.
Quality Inspection: Inspecting yarn or hides for defects (tears, discoloration, knots) and grading them according to industrial standards.
Setup & Maintenance: Adjusting machine settings for different material types and performing routine cleaning and lubrication.
2026 Update: Monitoring digital "Sustainability Dashboards" that track water usage and chemical recycling in the tanning and dyeing process.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is typically required.
Experience: Several months of on-the-job training are standard for entry-level operators. Previous experience as a labourer in a mill or tannery is an asset.
Language: CLB 4 or 5 is generally sufficient for most provincial pathways.
Safety: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS certification and training in handling industrial chemicals.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects over 3,000 job openings in this category through 2033. As Canada pivots toward "Smart Textiles" (fabrics with embedded sensors) and carbon-neutral leather processing, the need for technical operators who can manage digital manufacturing systems is projected to outpace the local supply of workers.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is characterized by "Good" prospects in Canada's traditional manufacturing heartlands:
Quebec (Montreal & Eastern Townships): Very Good prospects. This region remains the soul of Canada's textile industry, with a high concentration of technical textile mills and leather tanneries.
Ontario (GTA & Kitchener-Waterloo): Strong demand. Driven by the automotive sector’s need for processed leathers and advanced interior fabrics.

National Trend: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) indicates a "Balanced" market nationally, but high retirement rates among senior operators (over 35% of the workforce is 55+) are creating an urgent need for new international talent.
Why Work in Canada as a Textile/Hide Processing Operator?
Industrial Modernization: Work with 2026-grade technology, including automated dyeing vats, digital yarn-tension sensors, and eco-friendly leather tanning systems.
High-Value Exports: You are part of a sector that produces high-end materials for global aerospace, medical, and luxury fashion brands.
Path to PR: Frequently featured in PNP In-Demand lists for manufacturing and eligible for the 2026 TR to PR transition streams.
Stable Earnings: These technical processing roles often offer higher wages than general garment assembly due to the specialized machinery involved.
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): Textile and hide processing operators are a high-priority "Essential Manufacturing" occupation for this fast-track stream.
Quebec Regular Skilled Worker Program (RSWP): An excellent pathway for those working in Montreal's vibrant textile and leather sectors.
Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): This NOC is frequently eligible for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer in Ontario's manufacturing sector.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for semi-skilled manufacturing workers to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
